query: evidence on mitochondrial transfer between cells

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Electron Transport Chain -- Cellular Level
retrieved_docs.content: Complex IV, also known as cytochrome c oxidase, oxidizes cytochrome c and transfers the electrons to oxygen, the final electron carrier in aerobic cellular respiration. The cytochrome proteins a and a3, in addition to heme and copper groups in complex IV transfer the donated electrons to the bound dioxygen species, converting it into molecules of water. The free energy from the electron transfer causes 4 protons to move into the intermembrane space contributing to the proton gradient. Oxygen reduces via the following reaction: [13] [14] 2 cytochrome c(red) + ½O2 + 4 H+(matrix) -> 2 cytochrome c(ox) + 1 H2O + 2 H+(intermembrane)

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Metabolism -- Cellular Level
retrieved_docs.content: The chemical carrier of energy is called ATP. The synthesis of ATP takes place within an intracellular organelle bounded by an external membrane and an inner membrane. The dissociation of water to a molecule of hydrogen and a hydroxyl group that takes place in the internal milieu of the body is essential for the synthesis of ATP. The catabolic reactions, which this topic reviews later, release significant amounts of protons, most of which are transported to the mitochondria to produce ATP. These protons are transported through a series of complexes in the inner membrane of the mitochondria to activate an ATPase, using the energy released by the electron chain transport mechanism.

retrieved_docs.title: BAX Gene -- Cellular
retrieved_docs.content: The intrinsic pathway of apoptosis becomes activated via decreased survival signals. The Bax gene encodes BCL2L4 protein that, upon activation, heterodimerizes with Bcl2 family proteins and alters cellular mitochondria to induce cell death. [7] Bax protein is cytosolic in healthy cells with minimal binding and insertion into the outer mitochondrial membrane. Pro-survival signals, such as Bcl-xl and Bcl-2, ensure Bax does not accumulate on the outer mitochondrial membrane of healthy cells. [8] Although the complete mechanism by which Bax permeabilizes the MOM is unclear, it is known that Bax, upon activation, causes membrane depolarization and mitochondrial leaking of contents such as cytochrome c. [3] Morphological changes in the apoptotic cell include pyknosis (nuclear condensation and shrinkage), cell shrinkage, and membrane blebbing, leading to the release of vesicles containing cellular contents phagocytosed by surviving cells. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Electron Transport Chain -- Cellular Level
retrieved_docs.content: In the electron transport chain (ETC), the electrons go through a chain of proteins that increases its reduction potential and causes a release in energy. Most of this energy is dissipated as heat or utilized to pump hydrogen ions (H+) from the mitochondrial matrix to the intermembrane space and create a proton gradient. This gradient increases the acidity in the intermembrane space and creates an electrical difference with a positive charge outside and a negative charge inside. The ETC proteins in a general order are complex I, complex II, coenzyme Q, complex III, cytochrome C, and complex IV.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Electron Transport Chain -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The electron transport chain is a series of four protein complexes that couple redox reactions, creating an electrochemical gradient that leads to the creation of ATP in a complete system named oxidative phosphorylation. It occurs in mitochondria in both cellular respiration and in chloroplasts for photosynthesis. In the former, the electrons come from breaking down organic molecules, and energy is released. In the latter, the electrons enter the chain after being excited by light, and the energy released is used to build carbohydrates.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Lactate Dehydrogenase -- Cellular Level
retrieved_docs.content: Though LDH is predominantly a cytoplasmic enzyme, its mitochondrial presence is also demonstrated by various studies. The presence of a mitochondrial L-lactate dehydrogenase (mL-LDH) was confirmed in yeast, plant, and animals. [6] L-lactate, which is the substrate for mL-LDH, is transported to the mitochondria through L-lactate/H symporter and the L-lactate/pyruvate and L-lactate/oxaloacetate antiporters. Subsequently, mL-LDH facilitates the oxidation of L-lactate to pyruvate in the mitochondrial matrix. [7] Many cancer cells reprogram the mitochondrial processes to fulfill their higher demands for energy. Glycolysis becomes elevated in cancer cells, and hence, mL-LDH may be a player in accelerating oxidative phosphorylation. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Electron Transport Chain -- Cellular Level
retrieved_docs.content: Complex II, also known as succinate dehydrogenase, accepts electrons from succinate (an intermediate in the citric acid cycle) and acts as a second entry point to the ETC. When succinate oxidizes to fumarate, 2 electrons are accepted by FAD within complex II. FAD passes them to Fe-S clusters and then to coenzyme Q, similar to complex I. However; no protons are translocated across the membrane by complex II, therefore less ATP is produced with this pathway. [5] [6] Succinate + FAD -> Fumarate + 2 H+(matrix) + FADH2 FADH2 + CoQ -> FAD + CoQH2 Glycerol-3-Phosphate dehydrogenase and Acyl-CoA dehydrogenase also accept electrons from glycerol-3-P and fatty acyl-CoA, respectively. Inclusion of these protein complexes allows for the donation to the ETC by cytosolic NADH (glycerol-3-P acts as a shuttle to regenerate cytosolic NAD from NADH) and fatty acids undergoing beta-oxidation within the mitochondria (acyl-CoA is oxidized to enoyl-CoA in the first step, producing FADH2). [7] [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Ketone Metabolism -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: Fatty acids in the blood are converted to ketone bodies when insulin is low, and the fatty acid concentration is high. Fatty acyl CoA is transported into the liver mitochondria by the carnitine shuttle system. This system involves two transmembrane proteins to move fatty acyl CoA molecules across the mitochondrial membrane. The first protein is carnitine palmityl transferase I (CPT I), this protein on the cytosolic side of the mitochondrial membrane transfers the fatty acyl CoA across the outer membrane. During this process, a carnitine molecule is attached to the fatty acyl CoA molecule to make an acylcarnitine. The acylcarnitine is carried through the mitochondrial matrix by a transporter protein called carnitine/acylcarnitine translocase. At the inner mitochondrial membrane, the acylcarnitine molecule is converted back to acyl CoA and carnitine by CPT 2.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Fatty Acid Oxidation -- Cellular Level
retrieved_docs.content: Transportation of long-chain fatty acids into the mitochondrial matrix requires three enzymes in addition to acyl-CoA synthetase. The transport of fatty acyl-CoA across the outer mitochondrial membrane occurs by carnitine:palmitoyltransferase I (CPT I); this enzyme simultaneously converts fatty acylcarnitine. This step is heavily regulated by the energy status of the cell; malonyl-CoA levels rise during the synthesis of fatty acids and function to inhibit mitochondrial beta-oxidation at this point in the pathway. The enzymatic transportation and conversion completed by CPT I is the rate-limiting step of fatty acid oxidation in the mitochondria. Fatty acyl-carnitine molecules are then transported into the mitochondrial matrix in exchange for carnitine by carnitine:acylcarnitine translocase through an antiport mechanism. The pool of carnitine available to this transporter depends on the functioning of carnitine:palmitoyltransferase II (CPT II), which serves to convert acylcarnitine to fatty acyl-CoA, trapping the molecules within the mitochondrial matrix.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Electron Transport Chain -- Cellular Level
retrieved_docs.content: Complex III, also known as cytochrome c reductase, is made up of cytochrome b, Rieske subunits (containing two Fe-S clusters), and cytochrome c proteins. A cytochrome is a protein involved in electron transfer that contains a heme group. The heme groups alternate between ferrous (Fe2+) and ferric (Fe3+) states during the electron transfer. Because cytochrome c can only accept a single electron at a time, this process occurs in two steps (the Q cycle), in contrast to the single-step complex I and II pathways. Complex III also releases 4 protons into the intermembrane space at the end of a full Q cycle, contributing to the gradient. Cytochrome c then transfers the electrons one at a time to complex IV. [9] [10] [11] Q Cycle: Step 1 in the Q cycle involves ubiquinol (CoQH2) and ubiquinone (CoQ) binding to two separate sites on complex III. CoQH2 transfers each electron to a different path. One electron goes to Fe-S and then cytochrome c, while the second electron is transferred to cytochrome b and then to CoQ bound at the other site. While this occurs, 2 H+ ions are released into the intermembrane space, contributing to the proton gradient. CoQH2 is now oxidized to ubiquinone and dissociates from the complex. The CoQ bound at the second site enters a transitional CoQH- radical state from accepting one of the electrons. The second step of the cycle involves a repeat of the first: a new CoQH2 binds to the first site and transfers two electrons like before (and 2 more H+ ions released). Again, one electron passes to cytochrome c and one to cytochrome b, which this time works to reduce CoQH- to CoQH2 before it dissociates from complex III and can be recycled. In this way, one full cycle appears as follows: [12] 2 CoQH2(site 1) + CoQ(site 2) + 2 Cyt c(ox) + 2 H+(matrix) -> 2 CoQ(site 1) + CoQH2(site 2) + 2 Cyt c(red) + 4 H+(intermembrane)

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Cell Death -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Free cytoplasmic calcium increases mitochondrial permeability by opening a transitional pore. Exposure of mitochondrial contents artificially releases cytochrome c (Cyt c), an important factor in the electron transport chain as well as in apoptosis and cell death. Cyt c begins a cascade of cell injury by direct activation of caspases. This would be one way that necrosis and apoptosis begin to overlap, as caspase activation is a primary mechanism of apoptotic death. In necrotic cell death, the release of caspases is coincidental to cellular injury. In apoptosis, caspase activation is purposeful and initiated through a series of regulating factors.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Oxidative Phosphorylation -- Fundamentals
retrieved_docs.content: The mitochondrion consists of inner and outer membranes, both of which are composed of phospholipid bilayers and integral membrane proteins involved in enzymatic action and molecular transport. The inner membrane has inward-facing fold-like projections known as cristae that vastly increase the surface area of the membrane to maximize the amount of energy production. The protein complexes involved in the electron transport chain are studded along this membrane. The inner membrane envelops the matrix, which houses mitochondrial DNA, ribosomes, and a multitude of enzymes and metabolites. The space between the inner and outer membrane is known as the intermembrane space; this is the site of hydrogen ion deposition for the protein complexes in the electron transport chain. The increased hydrogen ion (H+ ion) concentration (and effectual decreased pH) generate a membrane potential across the inner mitochondrial membrane. [2] [4] [5] [6] [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Electron Transport Chain -- Cellular Level
retrieved_docs.content: ATP synthase, also called complex V, uses the ETC generated proton gradient across the inner mitochondrial membrane to form ATP. ATP-synthase contains up of F0 and F1 subunits, which act as a rotational motor system. F0 is hydrophobic and embedded in the inner mitochondrial membrane. It contains a proton corridor that is protonated and deprotonated repeatedly as H+ ions flow down the gradient from intermembrane space to matrix. The alternating ionization of F0 causes rotation, which alters the orientation of the F1 subunits. F1 is hydrophilic and faces the mitochondrial matrix. Conformational changes in F1 subunits catalyze the formation of ATP from ADP and Pi. For every 4 H+ ions, 1 ATP is produced. ATP-synthase can also be forced to run in reverse, consuming ATP to produce a hydrogen gradient, as is seen in some bacteria. [15] [16] [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Anaerobic Glycolysis -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: In mitochondria-containing cells, pyruvate can enter the citric acid cycle within the mitochondrial matrix and undergo oxidative phosphorylation . Aptly named due to its dependence on oxygen as the final electron acceptor, oxidative phosphorylation cannot take place in the absence of oxygen. Moreover, as the enzymes of both the citric acid cycle and electron transport chain are within the mitochondria, cells lacking mitochondria (e.g., erythrocytes) cannot rely on oxidative phosphorylation for energy production.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Lipolysis -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: Short and medium-chain fatty acids diffuse freely into the cytosol and mitochondria of cells. Long-chain fatty acids must undergo protein-mediated transport across the cell membrane into the cytosol via fatty acid translocase (FAT) or fatty acid-binding protein (FABP). Acyl-CoA synthase then converts the fatty acids to fatty acyl-CoA. The fatty acyl-CoA must now be transported into the mitochondria through the outer mitochondrial membrane and is done so by carnitine palmitoyltransferase-I (CPT-I) where it becomes fatty acyl-carnitine. The fatty acyl-carnitine is then transported across the inner membrane into the mitochondrial matrix by carnitine acyl-translocase (CAT) and converted back to fatty acyl-CoA by palmitoyltransferase-II (CPT-II) where it is now ready for oxidation. Beta oxidation

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Electron Transport Chain -- Cellular Level
retrieved_docs.content: Coenzyme Q, also known as ubiquinone (CoQ), is made up of quinone and a hydrophobic tail. Its purpose is to function as an electron carrier and transfer electrons to complex III. Coenzyme Q undergoes reduction to semiquinone (partially reduced, radical form CoQH-) and ubiquinol (fully reduced CoQH2) through the Q cycle. This process receives further elaboration under Complex III.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1628
retrieved_docs.content: Benzochinony is a parent quinone compound that gives rise to ubichinon, a specific derivative within the quinone family. Ubichinon, also known as coenzyme Q, inherits the core benzoquinone structure from benzochinony, linking the two molecules in biochemical pathways such as mitochondrial electron transport. Recognizing that ubichinon is a child of benzochinony clarifies the structural relationship and functional roles of these related quinones in cellular metabolism.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1330
retrieved_docs.content: Enzymes are biological catalysts that are organized into several major subclasses, each dedicated to a distinct type of chemical transformation. The subclass isomerases (isomerasy) catalyze the rearrangement of atoms within a molecule, converting it into its isomeric form. Ligases (ligasy) facilitate the joining of two molecules with the concomitant hydrolysis of a small pendant group such as ATP, forming new chemical bonds. Lyases (lyasy) break chemical bonds by means other than hydrolysis or oxidation, often generating a new double bond or a new ring structure. Oxidoreductases (oxidoreduktasy) mediate oxidation‑reduction reactions, transferring electrons between substrates. Transferases (transferasy) move functional groups, such as methyl or phosphate groups, from one molecule to another. Together, these enzyme subclasses encompass the primary mechanisms by which cells accelerate and regulate metabolic pathways, underscoring their central role in physiology and therapeutic targeting.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 314
retrieved_docs.content: Amino acids and derivatives comprise several biologically important compounds, including L‑carnitine, S‑adenosylmethionine, and glutamine, each of which is classified as a member of this chemical group. These molecules play key roles in cellular metabolism, energy production, methyl‑group transfer, and nitrogen balance, making them essential for normal physiological function and for the management of various metabolic disorders. In contrast, metreleptin, a recombinant leptin analog used to treat leptin deficiency and related metabolic conditions, does not belong to the amino acids and derivatives category; it is a protein‑based therapeutic rather than an amino‑acid‑derived compound. Recognizing the correct classification of these entities supports accurate understanding of their mechanisms of action and appropriate clinical application.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 1902
retrieved_docs.content: L‑karnitin, S‑adenosylmethionin, and glutamin are all classified as members of the broader category “Amino acids and derivatives.” This grouping includes both standard amino acids and related compounds that serve essential roles in metabolism, such as energy transport (L‑karnitin), methyl group donation (S‑adenosylmethionin), and nitrogen balance and protein synthesis (glutamin). Recognizing that these three substances belong to the Amino acids and derivatives class helps clinicians and researchers appreciate their shared biochemical context and informs their use in therapeutic and nutritional strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 412
retrieved_docs.content: FMRFamid is classified as a member of the modulátory membránového transportu (membrane transport modulators) group, indicating that it functions by influencing the activity of membrane transport proteins. The category farmakologické účinky – molekulární mechanismy (pharmacological effects – molecular mechanisms) is identified as a parent of modulátory membránového transportu, signifying that the molecular mechanisms underlying pharmacological effects encompass the actions of membrane transport modulators such as FMRFamid. This relationship highlights the role of membrane transport modulation in the broader context of drug action at the molecular level.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1917
retrieved_docs.content: المعالجة التنفسية is classified as a medical procedure on the respiratory tract, encompassing interventions that support or restore pulmonary function. Within this broader category, إيصال الأكسجين عبر غشاء خارج الجسم is identified as a child procedure, representing extracorporeal membrane oxygenation, a specialized technique that provides oxygenation and carbon‑dioxide removal outside the body. In clinical documentation, المعالجة التنفسية is also linked to the response category 关于预定行程的原因:印象:时间点:救护车运输:名义型, indicating that considerations of respiratory therapy may be recorded in the context of ambulance transport scheduling and related temporal impressions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 566
retrieved_docs.content: Enzymes are biological catalysts that are organized into several major subclasses, including isomerases, ligases, lyases, oxidoreductases, and transferases. Isomerases catalyze intramolecular rearrangements of chemical structures, ligases join two substrates together with the concomitant hydrolysis of a nucleoside triphosphate, lyases cleave bonds by means other than hydrolysis or oxidation, oxidoreductases mediate oxidation‑reduction reactions, and transferases move functional groups from one molecule to another. Beyond their essential metabolic functions, enzymes also play a central role in pharmacological effects, serving as the molecular mechanisms through which many drugs act, modulate physiological pathways, and influence therapeutic outcomes. Recognizing the classification of enzymes and their involvement in drug mechanisms is crucial for effective drug development and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 1771
retrieved_docs.content: Nuclear medicine procedure is classified as a therapeutic procedure (إجراء علاجي). Within the broader domain of therapeutic procedures, airway management (zajištění dýchacích cest) is identified as a specific type of therapeutic intervention that is closely linked to respiratory therapy (المعالجة التنفسية). Respiratory therapy functions as an overarching category that encompasses both airway management and more advanced techniques such as extracorporeal membrane oxygenation (إيصال الأكسجين عبر غشاء خارج الجسم), which delivers oxygen through an external membrane. These relationships indicate that while nuclear medicine procedure represents a distinct therapeutic modality, airway management and extracorporeal membrane oxygenation are specialized therapeutic procedures that fall under the larger umbrella of respiratory therapy, highlighting the interconnected nature of therapeutic strategies aimed at maintaining and supporting the respiratory system.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 2291
retrieved_docs.content: Chymotrypsin is classified as an enzyme, belonging to the broader enzyme family (enzymy). Within this family, enzymes are organized into major functional subclasses, each catalyzing distinct types of biochemical reactions. The enzyme class includes isomerases (isomerasy), which facilitate structural rearrangements of molecules; ligases (ligasy), which join two molecules together with the concomitant hydrolysis of a diphosphate bond; lyases (lyasy), which cleave bonds by means other than hydrolysis or oxidation, often forming a new double bond or a new ring structure; oxidoreductases (oxidoreduktasy), which mediate oxidation‑reduction reactions by transferring electrons between molecules; and transferases (transferasy), which move functional groups from one molecule to another. Understanding that chymotrypsin is part of this hierarchical enzyme system helps contextualize its specific proteolytic activity within the larger spectrum of enzymatic functions relevant to metabolism and therapeutic interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 450
retrieved_docs.content: Enzymy constitute a broad group of biological catalysts that are organized into several major subclasses based on the type of chemical reaction they facilitate. Among these subclasses are hydrolasy, which catalyze hydrolytic cleavage of bonds; isomerasy, which mediate the rearrangement of atoms within a molecule; ligasy, which join two molecules together with the concomitant hydrolysis of a diphosphate bond; lyasy, which break bonds by means other than hydrolysis or oxidation, often forming a new double bond or a new ring structure; oxidoreduktasy, which catalyze oxidation‑reduction (redox) reactions by transferring electrons between substrates; and transferasy, which transfer functional groups from one molecule to another. Recognizing that hydrolasy, isomerasy, ligasy, lyasy, oxidoreduktasy, and transferasy are all child categories of enzymy provides a foundational framework for understanding enzyme function, metabolic pathway organization, and the development of therapeutic agents that target specific enzymatic activities.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 1846
retrieved_docs.content: B [Preparations] are pharmaceutical or nutraceutical formulations whose parent compound is beta‑tocopherol, a specific isoform of vitamin E. Beta‑tocopherol functions as a potent antioxidant, protecting cellular membranes from oxidative damage and supporting various physiological processes. By using beta‑tocopherol as the foundational ingredient, B [Preparations] leverage its antioxidant properties to enhance therapeutic efficacy, improve stability of active ingredients, and contribute to overall patient health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1991
retrieved_docs.content: The mechanism of action for merkaptamin is currently described as an unknown cellular or molecular interaction, indicating that the precise cellular or molecular processes by which merkaptamin exerts its effects have not yet been elucidated.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 1389
retrieved_docs.content: Blokátory draslíkových kanálů and blokátory sodíkových kanálů are classified as modulátory membránového transportu, indicating that they function as agents that modulate membrane transport by blocking potassium and sodium channels, respectively. Additionally, FMRFamid is identified as a modulátor membránového transportu, further exemplifying a compound that influences membrane transport mechanisms. Together, these entities illustrate the broader category of membrane transport modulators, which encompasses agents that alter ion‑channel activity to affect cellular electrophysiology and therapeutic outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 2 -- Community 589
retrieved_docs.content: spans active therapeutic roles, supportive nutritional supplementation, and ancillary formulation functions, while its biochemical actions involve enzyme interactions and antioxidant activity, all grounded in its retinol‑acetate structural class.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 2 -- Community 561
retrieved_docs.content: and for transferasy, which is a subordinate component of enzymes. Proantokyanidiny is linked to taniny as a child‑parent relationship, indicating that taniny is a precursor or upstream molecule to proantokyanidiny. These interconnected classifications, hierarchical relationships, and therapeutic contraindications together illustrate the complex network of drug mechanisms, enzyme functions, and metabolic pathways relevant to informed patient care and pharmacological decision‑making.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 1 -- Community 33
retrieved_docs.content: antioxidant protection; water‑soluble B‑vitamins (niacin, calcium pantothenate) are combined with a broad spectrum of minerals and other vitamins to target cellular metabolism, energy production and vascular health. This integrated approach leverages biochemical relationships among vitamins, minerals and bioactive compounds to optimize patient health outcomes.
